{"covered_dims": {"R1.1": 0.6, "R1.2": 0.7, "R1.3": 0.9, "R1.4": 0.95, "R1.6": 0.7, "R1.7": 0.6}}

Summary: Revised the passage to remove unsupported novelty claims, soften overreach, fix internal inconsistencies, and tighten prose; appended a short list of remaining issues the authors should verify.

Revised text:
---
We present ClariSeek, a conversational retrieval system that asks clarifying questions when user queries are ambiguous, rather than committing to a single ranked list and leaving disambiguation to the user through query reformulation. Building on a line of work on mixed-initiative and clarification-based search (Aliannejadi et al., 2019; Zamani et al., 2020), ClariSeek tightly couples clarification with the retrieval loop: clarifying questions are issued before final ranking, and user responses are folded back into the retrieval state.

The architecture has three components: a query ambiguity detector, a clarification-question generator, and a re-ranker that conditions on user responses. The ambiguity detector applies a fine-tuned transformer head to BM25-retrieved candidates and estimates facet entropy over the candidate set; when entropy exceeds a learned threshold, the system invokes the question generator in place of returning results. The generator is trained on (query, facet, question) triples extracted from the Qulac dataset (Aliannejadi et al., 2019) and from session logs released with the TREC CAsT 2020 track.

We evaluate ClariSeek on the ClariQ benchmark and on a held-out subset of MS MARCO Conversational. Against a BM25 baseline that returns top-1 results without clarification, ClariSeek improves nDCG@5 by 11.4 points. Against an ablation without the response-aware re-ranker, the full system improves nDCG@5 by 2.1 points.

Our contribution is an end-to-end integration of facet-aware retrieval with in-loop clarification, in which the decision to clarify, the generated question, and the post-response re-ranking share a single retrieval state rather than being handled by a separate dialog manager bolted onto a QA stack.
---

Remaining issues to verify before submission:

1. The original claim that ClariSeek is "the first system to break from this paradigm by issuing clarifying questions before retrieval" is not defensible. Aliannejadi et al. (2019), Zamani et al. (2020, "Generating Clarifying Questions for Information Retrieval"), and the ClariQ shared task (2020) all predate this work and propose pre-retrieval clarification. The closing "first end-to-end approach… at production scale" claim is similarly overreaching — "production scale" is not evidenced anywhere in the passage. I removed both first-claims and rephrased the contribution as an integration claim; please confirm this reflects your actual delta.

2. There is an internal inconsistency: paragraph 1 says the system issues clarifying questions "before retrieval," but paragraph 2 says the ambiguity detector operates "over BM25-retrieved candidates" — so retrieval has already happened. I rephrased paragraph 1 to "before final ranking," which is consistent with the architecture described; verify this is what you mean.

3. The 11.4-point gain over "vanilla BM25 without clarification" is a weak comparison — BM25 is not a clarification-capable baseline, so the delta conflates the value of clarification with the value of neural re-ranking. Consider adding a comparison to a prior clarification system (e.g., NeuralCQ, BERT-based ClariQ baselines) so the headline number isolates your contribution. If such a comparison exists elsewhere in the paper, the abstract should reference it.

4. The 2.1-point ablation gain is reported without variance or significance; if the full-system-vs.-no-reranker comparison is close to noise, the framing should be softened.

5. "Facet entropy" and "learned threshold" are introduced without definition. If this passage is the abstract or intro, that is acceptable; if it is the method section, both terms need a sentence of grounding.

6. Citation coverage is thin. Qulac and CAsT are cited, but ClariQ, MS MARCO Conversational, and the prior clarification-question literature (Zamani et al., Rao & Daumé, Kumar & Callan) are not. Adding these would also weaken the appearance of novelty-inflation.

Uncertainty: I have not verified the exact publication years or claim scope of Zamani et al. 2020 or the ClariQ 2020 shared task against the literature in this session; the recommendation in point 1 rests on widely known prior work but you should confirm the specific citations before incorporating them.
